title: Defining and Calling a Function
coreIdea: A function is a named block of code; `def` defines it, and using its name runs it.
plainEnglish: A function is a chunk of code you give a name.
You write it once with `def`, then run it later by calling its name.
Defining a function does not run it. Calling it does.
This lets you write a set of steps once and trigger them whenever you want.
Under the hood, Python stores the function as an object in memory, just like it stores a number or a string in a variable.
The name `greet` is really a label that points at that stored block of code.
When you write `greet()`, the parentheses are the signal that says "run the thing this name points to, right now."

metaphor.title: 🧭 Think of it like…
body: Think of a button on a microwave labelled "Popcorn".
Someone set up what that button does once.
After that, anyone just presses it — they do not rebuild the settings every time.

deepSections.title: Defining is not the same as calling
deepSections.teaser: writing the function vs. actually running it
body: `def greet():` tells Python: here is a recipe called `greet`.
Python reads it, remembers it, and moves on. Nothing prints yet.
The function only runs when you write its name with parentheses: `greet()`.
A common beginner surprise: you define a function, run the file, and nothing happens — because you never called it.

deepSections.title: The shape of a definition
deepSections.teaser: def, a name, parentheses, a colon, and an indented body
body: Start with `def`, then a name you choose, then `()` and a `:`.
The lines that belong to the function are indented underneath.
Indentation is how Python knows where the function starts and ends.

deepSections.title: The name and the parentheses do two different jobs
deepSections.teaser: why `greet` alone does nothing, but `greet()` runs the code
body: Writing the name on its own — just `greet` — only points at the function. It does not run it.
The parentheses `()` are the part that actually triggers the code to run. People call this 'invoking' or 'calling'.
This is a real and common bug: someone writes `greet` instead of `greet()` and wonders why nothing happens.
Python does not warn you. The line `greet` is valid — it just evaluates the function and throws the result away, so it looks like nothing happened.
Tip to remember it: a name with no parentheses is like reading the recipe; a name with parentheses is like cooking it.

keyTerms.def: A named block of code you can run whenever you want.
The keyword that defines a new function.
Running a function by writing its name followed by ().
The indented lines underneath the def line — the actual steps the function runs.
The blank space at the start of a line; in Python it shows which lines belong inside the function.
The round brackets () after a function name; writing them is what tells Python to run the function.

codeExamples.caption: Define once, call twice — the second call is free
The name alone does nothing — only the parentheses run it
codeExamples.source:
def greet():
    print("Welcome!")

greet
print("Still here, nothing printed above")
greet()

challenge.prompt: Define a function called greet that prints hi, then call it once.
challenge.hint: Write the function with def greet():, indent print("hi") underneath, then call greet() on its own line.

knowledgeCheck.q: You write `def greet():` with `print("hi")` indented underneath, then run the file. Nothing prints. Why?

options.text: You defined the function but never called it with greet()
Python ran the function but hid the output
The function name greet is not allowed in Python

knowledgeCheck.feedback: Defining a function only stores it. You have to call it — write greet() on its own line — to actually run the code inside.

knowledgeCheck.q: What is the difference between writing `greet` and writing `greet()`?

options.text: greet just points at the function; greet() actually runs it
There is no difference; both run the function
greet runs the function and greet() defines it

knowledgeCheck.feedback: The parentheses do the work. `greet` alone just refers to the function; `greet()` calls it and runs the code inside.

knowledgeCheck.q: In Python, how does the language know which lines belong inside a function?

options.text: The lines are indented underneath the def line
The lines are wrapped in curly braces { }
The lines each end with the word end

knowledgeCheck.feedback: Python uses indentation. The indented lines under `def greet():` form the function's body. Curly braces are used by other languages like Java, not Python.

caseStudy.title: Instagram's "like" button is one function, called billions of times
body: When you tap the heart on a photo, the same block of code runs every time.
Engineers wrote that "record a like" logic once, inside a function.
It is then called for every user, on every post — Instagram has reported over 4.2 billion likes per day.
Nobody rewrites the like logic for each tap; the function name is just called again and again.
This is why functions matter at scale: one definition, fixed and tested in a single place, can serve billions of calls.
caseStudy.bridge: Define a useful action once, call it as often as you need — that is exactly what you just did with greet().

takeaways: `def` defines a function; the indented lines are its body.
Defining a function does not run it.
Calling it by name — `greet()` — is what makes it run.
The parentheses are the part that triggers the code; the name alone does nothing.
